perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to the provisi ons specified in this Agreement and, as applicable, the Health Insurance Portability and Accoun tability Act (HIPAA) , the Michigan Identity Theft Protection Act, and all other applicable public health, research, and confidentiality laws .
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to the provisions specified in this Agreement and, as applicable, the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), the Michigan Identity Theft Protection Act, and any other laws they deem relevant.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all other applicable public health, research, and confidentiality laws' to 'any other laws they deem relevant,' the contract introduces ambiguity and the potential for parties to disregard relevant laws. This conflicts with the intention of ensuring compliance with all applicable laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contractual obligation to comply with state and federal laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Michigan law MCL 445.61 pertains to fair and equitable practices in business, this modification contradicts the spirit of ensuring all applicable laws are followed, potentially allowing businesses to selectively choose which laws they follow, violating the intent of the law to have equitable practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.61 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.61 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 452 of 2004 Previous Section Next Section IDENTITY THEFT PROTECTION ACT (EXCERPT) Act 452 of 2004 445.61 Short title. Sec. 1. This act shall be known and may be cited as the "identity theft protection act". History: 2004, Act 452 , Eff. Mar. 1, 2005
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by changing 'all other applicable public health, research, and confidentiality laws' to 'any other laws they deem relevant.' This creates ambiguity and allows parties to disregard relevant laws, undermining the original intention of ensuring compliance with all applicable laws. The law explanation suggests this could violate principles of fair and equitable practices. While the scraped snippet primarily defines the Identity Theft Protection Act, the Perturbation Explanation highlights that the modified text now allows parties to selectively follow laws, creating a situation where they might not adhere to the Identity Theft Protection Act or other relevant laws if they don't 'deem' them relevant. This directly undermines the purpose of ensuring that 'all other applicable...laws' are followed, which is a crucial aspect of legal and ethical business practices.

perturbation.original_text: Data in the Michigan Trauma Registry will be dis closed to Regional Trauma Networks through access to the Michigan Trauma Registry database. Individuals gra nted access to the Michigan Trauma Registry database will be required to execute user agreements with MDHHS .
perturbation.changed_text: Data in the Michigan Trauma Registry will be disclosed to Regional Trauma Networks through access to the Michigan Trauma Registry database. Individuals granted access to the Michigan Trauma Registry database may, at MDHHS's discretion, be required to execute user agreements with MDHHS.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be required' to 'may, at MDHHS's discretion, be required' introduces uncertainty about the mandatory nature of user agreements. User agreements are essential for data security and compliance with HIPAA and other privacy laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(C) requires implementation of security awareness and training for all workforce members, making user agreements discretionary undermines this provision, potentially leading to security breaches and non-compliance with HIPAA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: the provisions of the Security Rule. 73 Documentation. A regulated entity must maintain documentation required for written policies and procedures implemented to comply with the Security Rule and actions, activities, or assessments required by the Security Rule to be documented until six years after the later of: 1) the date of the document’s creation or 2) the date the document was last in effect. 74 Availability. A regulated entity must make the written policies and procedures it implements to comply with the Security Rule and its documentation of required actions, activities, and assessments available to those persons responsible for implementing the procedures described in the documentation. 75 Updates. A regulated entity must periodically review and update its documentation in response to environmental or organizational changes that affect the security of ePHI. 76 Endnotes [1]Subtitle F of title II of HIPAA (Pub. perturbation.justification: The change from 'will be required' to 'may, at MDHHS's discretion, be required' introduces ambiguity regarding the mandatory nature of user agreements. The Law Explanation states that 45 CFR § 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(C) necessitates security awareness and training for all workforce members, and discretionary user agreements undermine this provision. Scraped Law Snippet 2 makes several references to 45 CFR 164.308, specifically citing it in relation to security measures (e.g., footnote 45 mentions 45 CFR 164.308(a)(5)(i)). This suggests that weakening the requirement for user agreements potentially weakens the enforcement of security awareness and training, thereby contradicting HIPAA regulations, because the user agreements are no longer necessarily required.

perturbation.original_text: Report to the Reporting Entity within ten (10) business days of discovery any actual or suspected inappropriate use, disclosure , or breach of information from the Michigan Trauma Registry that involves data submitted under this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: Report to the Reporting Entity within a reasonable time frame of discovery any actual inappropriate use, disclosure, or breach of information from the Michigan Trauma Registry that involves data submitted under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'ten (10) business days' to 'a reasonable time frame' introduces ambiguity and subjectivity. The original text gives a clear timeline for reporting, crucial for timely corrective actions and compliance. This contradicts established data breach notification laws that require specificity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Michigan Identity Theft Protection Act - Data Breach Notification
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 445.72(1) mandates that a data breach needs to be addressed in a reasonable timeframe and reported promptly to the residents. Changing 'ten (10) business days' to a reasonable time frame is vague and contradicts the provision of data breach notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.72 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.72 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 452 of 2004 Previous Section Next Section IDENTITY THEFT PROTECTION ACT (EXCERPT) Act 452 of 2004 445.72 Notice of security breach; requirements. Sec. 12. (1) Unless the person or agency determines that the security breach has not or is not likely to cause substantial loss or injury to, or result in identity theft with respect to, 1 or more residents of this state, a person or agency that owns or licenses data that are included in a database that discovers a security breach, or receives notice of a security breach under subsection (2), shall provide a notice of the security breach to each resident of this state who meets 1 or more of the following: (a) That resident's unencrypted and unredacted personal information was accessed and acquired by an unauthorized person. (b) That resident's personal information was accessed and acquired in encrypted form by a person with unauthorized access to the encryption key. (2) Unless the person or agency determines that the security breach has not or is not likely to cause substantial loss or injury to, or result in identity theft with respect to, 1 or more residents of this state, a person or agency that maintains a database that includes data that the person or agency does not own or license that discovers a breach of the security of the database shall provide a notice to the owner or licensor of the information of the security breach. (3) In determining whether a security breach is not likely to cause substantial loss or injury to, or result in identity theft with respect to, 1 or more residents of this state under subsection (1) or (2), a person or agency shall act with the care an ordinarily prudent person or agency in like position would exercise under similar circumstances. (4) A person or agency shall provide any notice required under this section without unreasonable delay. A person or agency may delay providing notice without violating this su
perturbation.justification: The change from 'ten (10) business days' to 'a reasonable time frame' for reporting data breaches introduces unacceptable ambiguity, directly contradicting the legal requirement for timely notification. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL § 445.72(4), explicitly states that 'A person or agency shall provide any notice required under this section without unreasonable delay.' Replacing a specific timeframe with a subjective one undermines the mandate of 'without unreasonable delay.' This creates a legal contradiction because the modified text weakens the enforceability of the notification requirement, potentially leading to non-compliance and delayed response to data breaches. The original text provided a clear standard that could be enforced, while the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: Destroy all originals and copies of potentially identifiable information , in any format , in accordance with industry standards when no longer needed. This includes, but is not limited to: magnetic tape s, micro di sk files, paper records, etc.
perturbation.changed_text: Destroy all originals and copies of potentially identifiable information, in any format, when no longer needed. Specific methods of destruction will be determined by MDHHS.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'in accordance with industry standards' and adding 'Specific methods of destruction will be determined by MDHHS' allows for the possibility of data destruction methods that do not meet industry standards. This puts the organization at risk of non-compliance with data security regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 45 CFR § 164.310(d)(2)(i) states that covered entities must implement policies and procedures to address the final disposition of electronic protected health information and/or the hardware or electronic media on which it is stored. Removing 'in accordance with industry standards' opens up the possibility of methods that dont meet the standard, which is a violation.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates data destruction 'in accordance with industry standards', whereas the modified text delegates the determination of destruction methods to MDHHS, potentially allowing for methods that do not meet industry standards. The Law Explanation points to 45 CFR § 164.310(d)(2)(i), which addresses the final disposition of electronic protected health information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 45 CFR 164.310 within its notes ([55] See 45 CFR 164.310(d).), confirming its relevance. The removal of 'in accordance with industry standards' directly contradicts the implication of the law requiring a standard of care, creating a legal risk of non-compliance by potentially allowing methods that fall below accepted practices. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' perturbation type definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions.
